utterances: The current NookSack Tribal Council would not return multiple interview requests.
And the federal government wouldn't agree to interviews because of pending litigation.

But the federal government has sent letters to NookSack Tribe saying that if the council does not hold a fair election this month, it could come in and take control of tribal services such as the NookSack's law enforcement and medical programs.
That's very rare at this point in history.
David Wilkins is a professor of American Indian studies at the University of Minnesota.
He says about 80 tribes have disenrolled members.
But he's never seen a case like the NookSack.
This may well be the strongest case where the federal government has finally stepped in to finally stand up for the rights of those individuals facing disenrollment.
Wilkins says disenrollments really started happening in the 1990s.
That's when tribal casinos became a factor.
The fewer members in a tribe, the more money each member gets.
But NookSack tribal members don't get big casino payouts.
Their casino's too small, says Michelle Roberts.
She's among the disenrolled.
But she points out tribal council members can often make six-figure salaries.
She says what this is really about is a fight over control of tribal government.
Her 300-member family makes up a strong voting bloc in the 2,000-member tribe.
Our family is huge.
And they don't like the power that we had when coming in.
Now many people in Roberts' family are struggling without tribal benefits.
They've been told they aren't NookSack because they couldn't provide some birth certificates from generations ago.
It's very draining to tell somebody that you don't belong somewhere, that your ancestry doesn't mean anything.
It takes your identity away.
And the struggle of identity and sovereignty could become a bigger issue if the federal government steps in and takes over tribal services.
For NPR News, I'm Emily Fox on the NookSack reservation.
(SOUNDBITE OF SPANISH GOLD SONG, ""SOUTH OF NOWHERE"")
Living by the ocean might sound nice.
In the time of climate change, it can be risky.
As sea levels rise, people who live near the coast have tough choices - try to fortify their homes, move to higher ground or just leave.
Sam Evans-Brown, host of New Hampshire Public Radio's podcast ""Outside/In,"" has this story about homeowners dealing with these decisions.
Nahant, Mass., is a rocky crescent moon of land out in the Atlantic Ocean just north of Boston.
For its entire history, it's been at the mercy of the ocean.
To get to the town back in the 1800s, you'd cross a long, beautiful beach at low tide that connected it to the mainland.
At high tide, you had to take a boat.
These days, there's a four-lane road built on that beach, and it sits just a few feet above the water.
(SOUNDBITE OF KNOCKING)
Hi, how you doing?
I'm doing well.
How are you?
Dave Lazarro is a retired bartender and a woodworker.
D.
LAZARRO: When the weather's good and in the summer, there was no prettier, nicer place.
We have a private beach.
We have a great view.
The shipping lanes of Boston come right in front of our house, so all the big ships that come in, we can see.
And I love it.
I love my house.
The house Lazarro shares with his wife, Chris, is full of his carvings.
It's kind of a comforting nautical clutter.
He and Chris have lived on Willow Road for nearly 50 years in a house that at high tide is a stone's throw from the water.
More than 120 million people live in counties on the United States Coast, but anyone living on the water knows you get the beautiful summer days but also the brutal storms.
D.
LAZARRO: When a storm comes in, the house shakes.
You can literally feel it.
Back during the Blizzard of 1978, Dave Lazarro tried to ride out the storm in his home.
D.
LAZARRO: When we finally decided to leave, the current was so strong in front of the house that I had to take - I had a boat at the time, and I had an anchor in the cellar.
And I took the anchor, swung it around, threw it over across the street and hooked it onto the chain-link fence.
And we use that as security in case we got swept.
As the seas rise, climate scientists predict devastation like this will become more and more common.
Higher seas will mean even a less-powerful storm could push the tides up over Nahant's seawalls.
This is a problem that Sam Merrill spends his days grappling with.
He works with a firm that helps communities protect themselves from storms.
The unfortunate thing is, what we're really facing in so many of these coastal areas is what you can rightly call an extinction threat - the extinction of a community.
We don't know how to deal with extinction.
It's not really a conversation in our public sphere.
One independent group of climate scientists estimates that about 12 million people could be at risk.
That's 12 million people who need answers.
You always have three options - fortify, accommodate and relocate.
If this sounds like a simple choice, try spending some time in a place like Nahant.
Just a few doors down from Dave Lazarro lives Ken Carangelo.
He's single, no kids and an executive at a big company in the film industry.
In front of his house, there's an imposing seawall, tall enough that you can't reach the top of it from the beach and deep enough that there's another 10 feet buried in the sand.
And everything that's down there has a solid footing and Kevlar-coated rebar so the salt doesn't get to it as much.
That wall would be option one - a fortification.
Ken bought this house in 2008, but he says the seawall cost the previous owners more than $200,000.
And, Carrie--but I did want to get back to that point you were making.
This is your husband.
Average American is something to look up to.
No, you absolutely can.
I mean when a parent is sanctioned, the whole family suffers.
There's less resources for the entire family.
But still I think it was an important distinction in the California Welfare Plan that they maintain the safety net for kids.
I have yet to see a legal sanction.
I see disabled mothers being sanctioned.
I've seen mothers in wheelchairs be sanctioned.

I've seen mothers who are domestic violence victims who are on the run and hiding from the father of their children who's threatening to kill them be sanctioned for being unable to come to a mandatory welfare to work appointment that was scheduled without her consent or input and that was at the same time as a hearing for a restraining order for domestic violence.
So when this one mother called the caseworker to request the appointment be rescheduled, she was told I can't help you and she was sanctioned.
And mind you, under the old system, the states got cash rewards for caseload reduction.
So when they sanction the parent, they got a bonus for that.
Under the new rules, if they continue to sanction these parents, they're going to get hit with a $185 million federal penalty.
These proposals would basically take away all the state's responsibility for having to help that family at all.

And just to give you a small idea of how bad the system is currently working at helping families and serving the needs of women who are largely domestic violence victims, we looked at the state's own data on this California Department of Social Services Web site and they are providing domestic violence counseling and services to less than 1 percent of women on welfare statewide, not even 1 percent.
But why don't you step into another role just for a second.

Suppose you're a state executive and you're looking at this federal policy that says that the state could lose hundreds of millions of dollars in federal funds if the parents don't meet these minimum work requirements and you've got a state that's already in the red budget-wise.
What would you do?
I would devise a system that assesses what each parent needs when they come in the system, which the system's already supposed to do now.
And say oh, you've got mental health issues; you're fleeing domestic violence.
You don't have a high school diploma.

Let's get you into an education and training program along with some counseling, so that for the next 12 months you can get a stable base for you and your kids to develop a plan to get your family out of poverty.

And then at that 12-month mark, let's look at your plan again, help you get a part-time job while you continue to be in school and engage in the counseling activities that you need to address all your family's needs.
And many of these needs are the very reasons why these women came to the welfare system in the first place.
I was working at a $4.75-an-hour job.
I was looking at seven drug felony charges at one point because it was so depressing to be a single mother, homeless, making $4.75 an hour.
I was self-medicating and selling a little bit of the side to help make ends meet.

And for someone like me to have the opportunity that I did to go to community college, to get my education and to make a difference now in my community while I make a self-sufficient income, this is a dream come true.
And this is what federal and state welfare policy could do in this country today if the government would stop trying to penalize people for being poor.
Well, Diana Spatz, thank you so much.
You're welcome.
Diana Spatz is executive director of Lifetime, a welfare advocacy group based on Oakland, California.
And she spent more than 10 years on welfare.
